brief.name: Cleaning Pricing Calculator
brief.target_user: Independent house cleaners and small cleaning teams who need a faster way to quote jobs without underpricing labor.
brief.core_problem: Cleaners lose time and margin when every estimate starts from scratch, especially when service type, frequency, and add-ons change the scope.
primary_intent: how much should I charge for house cleaning
brief.tool_output: Working estimate range, average quote anchor, pricing notes, and a printable summary.
risk_notes: frame outputs as planning support, not guaranteed market pricing

homepage.hero_title: Cleaning pricing calculator for independent cleaners
homepage.hero_intro: Build a working estimate fast, explain your number clearly, and stop rebuilding every quote from scratch.
homepage.lede: This site pairs a practical estimator with scope guides, pricing comparisons, and a plain-English checklist for cleaning teams that need speed without sloppy math.
homepage.value_intro: The goal is not fake precision. The goal is a faster quoting workflow that still respects labor, frequency, and add-on scope.
value_points: Get a fast range before you write or text a quote.
Use repeatable pricing notes instead of rethinking the whole job every time.
Move from quick math to a cleaner estimate workflow with one checklist.
homepage.next_step: Start with the estimator, then use the recurring pricing and service-type guides to tighten the quote before you send it.
workflow_steps: Enter the square footage and room count.
Choose service type, frequency, and add-ons.
Copy or print the result before sending the quote.
homepage.who_for_body: Use this when you are a solo cleaner or a small team that needs a fast working estimate before walking the job, texting a client back, or packaging recurring service.
homepage.tool_preview_body: The calculator returns a working range, an average quoting anchor, and scope notes you can keep in your head while you refine the final number.
homepage.faq_preview_intro: These are the practical pricing questions that usually slow cleaners down in the middle of quoting.
homepage.comparison_preview_intro: Deep clean, move-out, recurring, and standard work should not all be quoted the same way.
homepage.resource_preview_intro: Use the checklist when you want the same quoting rhythm across texts, calls, and walkthrough notes.

tool_page.title: Cleaning pricing estimator
tool_page.intro: Choose the job shape, add the room count, and get a working price range before you commit to the final quote.
tool_page.inputs_intro: Keep the first pass lean so you can quote quickly and refine only where the job actually changes.
tool_page.result_intro: The result is a working range, not a fake guarantee.
tool_page.result_detail: Use the average target as your anchor, then adjust for travel, condition, pet hair, lockouts, stairs, or client-specific requests.
trust_paragraphs: The estimator is designed to speed up the first pricing pass, not replace judgment about condition, travel, or unusual labor.
If the job includes heavy buildup, clutter, or access friction, move upward from the range instead of pretending the baseline math covers everything.
tool_page.trust_callout: Static-first rule: the estimator runs fully in the browser, so you can use it on a phone between walkthroughs or while replying to a quote request.
next_steps: Use the average target as your quoting anchor.
Adjust upward for difficult access or heavy condition.
Copy or print the summary before you send the quote.

about_page.title: Why cleaning estimates break when the workflow is too loose
about_page.lede: A quote process feels slow when every inquiry forces you to recalculate the same scope decisions from memory.
about_page.takeaway: Standardize the first pass and you protect both your time and your margin.
about_page.detail: Once you separate standard, deep, and move-out work, then layer in frequency and add-ons, pricing becomes easier to explain and easier to repeat.
about_page.next_action: Run the estimator first, then use the guides to tighten the number before you send the final quote.

feedback_page.title: What should the next cleaning tool solve?
feedback_page.intro: This release stays static-first, so the feedback route is a notes page that defines the best next signals to collect once the niche earns a lightweight intake flow.
prompts: Which part of quoting still feels slow after using the estimator?
Do you need a walkthrough checklist, a route planner, or a quote follow-up template next?
Which service type creates the most pricing uncertainty in your market?
feedback_page.callout: Keep this page honest until a real winner earns a form integration.

faq_topics.question: How much should I charge for house cleaning?
faq_topics.short_answer: Start with a repeatable baseline tied to square footage, room count, service type, and frequency, then adjust for condition and access instead of guessing from memory.
faq_topics.reason: A fast baseline keeps you from underpricing busy weeks and overthinking easy jobs.
faq_topics.next_step: Run the estimator with the current job details before you text back a quote.

faq_topics.question: Should deep cleaning cost more than standard cleaning?
faq_topics.short_answer: Yes. Deep cleaning takes longer, includes more detail work, and usually needs a larger first-visit buffer than standard recurring service.
faq_topics.reason: Treating deep cleans like standard maintenance work compresses your margin fast.
faq_topics.next_step: Compare standard and deep-clean guidance before bundling both into one price.

faq_topics.question: Should I discount recurring cleaning?
faq_topics.short_answer: Usually yes, but only because recurring homes are easier to maintain over time, not because the work is magically cheap.
faq_topics.reason: A small recurring discount can improve retention while still protecting your labor rate.
faq_topics.next_step: Use the frequency selector in the estimator to see how the quote range changes.

faq_topics.question: Do add-ons need separate pricing?
faq_topics.short_answer: They should. Fridge, oven, interior windows, and laundry-style extras create extra labor that should not disappear inside the base price.
faq_topics.reason: Clear add-on pricing keeps the quote cleaner for both you and the client.
faq_topics.next_step: Toggle the add-ons in the estimator before packaging the final number.

faq_topics.question: Should small homes have a minimum price?
faq_topics.short_answer: Usually yes. Very small jobs still carry travel time, setup time, and communication overhead.
faq_topics.reason: Pure square-foot math can underprice short visits unless you respect the minimum effort required to show up.
faq_topics.next_step: Use the estimator, then compare the result to your minimum visit target.

faq_topics.question: How do I price move-out cleaning?
faq_topics.short_answer: Price move-out work higher than a standard clean because empty homes expose more detail work and clients often expect a near-reset condition.
faq_topics.reason: Move-out cleaning carries tighter expectations and less margin for missed details.
faq_topics.next_step: Use the move-out setting in the estimator and then review the comparison guide.

guides.title: Cleaning pricing baseline guide for solo cleaners
guides.lede: Build a fast baseline first so every quote starts from the same logic instead of the mood of the moment.
guides.takeaway: Stable quote math protects both your speed and your margin.
guides.detail: Separate square footage, room count, service type, and extras before you touch final judgment calls like travel or condition.
guides.next_action: Open the estimator and check your next job against the baseline before you adjust the final number.

guides.title: Deep cleaning price guide for first-visit jobs
guides.lede: Deep cleaning is not just more time. It is a different scope with more detail work and more room for underpricing.
guides.takeaway: Quote deep cleans as a scope tier, not a small add-on.
guides.detail: First visits often include buildup, reset work, and client expectations that do not belong inside a maintenance rate.
guides.next_action: Compare the deep-clean result to the standard-clean result before you send the quote.

guides.title: Recurring cleaning pricing workflow that protects your margin
guides.lede: Recurring work can justify a discount, but only when the scope really becomes easier to maintain over time.
guides.takeaway: Discount the rhythm, not the labor value.
guides.detail: Weekly and biweekly service usually reduce reset time, while monthly work can still feel closer to a light deep clean.
guides.next_action: Use the frequency selector to see how the working range changes before you offer the recurring rate.

guides.title: Add-on pricing guide for fridge, oven, windows, and extras
guides.lede: Add-ons should stay visible in the quote so the client understands the price and you understand the margin.
guides.takeaway: Clear extras are easier to sell and easier to protect.
guides.detail: Fridge interiors, oven degreasing, inside windows, and laundry extras all carry time that disappears when buried in the base rate.
guides.next_action: Toggle the add-ons in the estimator and copy the result before sending the quote.

guides.title: Move-out cleaning estimate workflow for higher-expectation jobs
guides.lede: Empty homes often look simple, but they expose every missed detail and tend to carry tighter client expectations.
guides.takeaway: Move-out pricing needs a bigger labor buffer than maintenance cleaning.
guides.detail: The lack of furniture does not automatically make the work easier when the condition is rough or the turnover deadline is tight.
guides.next_action: Use the move-out option in the estimator and check the range against your condition notes.

guides.title: Cleaning quote text template workflow after the estimate is ready
guides.lede: The quote itself should be short, clear, and tied to the scope decisions you already made in the estimator.
guides.takeaway: A cleaner quote message reduces back-and-forth and protects the agreed scope.
guides.detail: Restate the service type, frequency, extras, and final number so the estimate does not get fuzzy once the client replies.
guides.next_action: Copy the estimate summary and pair it with your normal quote template.

comparisons.title: Standard vs deep cleaning pricing and when each should cost more
comparisons.lede: Standard and deep cleaning are different jobs. Mixing them into one loose quote usually punishes the cleaner.
comparisons.option_one_body: Use a standard-cleaning rate when the home is already maintained and the visit mainly protects the current condition.
comparisons.option_two_body: Use a higher deep-cleaning rate when the home needs reset work, heavier detail attention, or buildup removal.
comparisons.decision_shortcut: If the home needs reset labor before maintenance can begin, price it as a deep clean.

comparisons.title: One-time vs recurring cleaning pricing and where the discount belongs
comparisons.lede: One-time work and recurring work create different labor patterns, so the quote should reflect the rhythm instead of flattening everything.
comparisons.option_one_body: Use a higher one-time rate when each visit starts cold and the home may need more reset work.
comparisons.option_two_body: Use a recurring rate when consistent service lowers setup time and makes the home easier to maintain over time.
comparisons.decision_shortcut: If repeated visits genuinely reduce effort, discount the recurring service slightly instead of discounting the one-time job.

comparisons.title: Flat-rate vs hourly cleaning pricing for small teams
comparisons.lede: Both pricing models can work, but flat-rate quoting usually scales better once your scope math is consistent.
comparisons.option_one_body: Use flat-rate pricing when you know the scope well enough to package the work cleanly and avoid constant time math during the quote.
comparisons.option_two_body: Use hourly pricing when the scope is uncertain, access is messy, or the client keeps changing the work list.
comparisons.decision_shortcut: Default to flat-rate when the scope is stable and switch to hourly when uncertainty is still too high.

resource.title: Cleaning pricing checklist
resource.lede: Download a portable quoting checklist before your next estimate call or walkthrough.
resource.resource_contents: A plain-English checklist covering baseline math, service type, frequency, add-ons, minimum pricing, and quote follow-up.
resource.usage_steps: Start with the estimator, check the scope decisions, confirm add-ons, and copy the summary before sending the quote.
resource.next_action: Pair the checklist with the estimator so your quote rhythm stays consistent across inquiries.

pages.title: Recurring cleaning pricing workflow that protects your margin
pages.description: Recurring work can justify a discount, but only when the scope really becomes easier to maintain over time.
pages.heroTitle: Recurring cleaning pricing workflow that protects your margin
pages.heroIntro: Recurring work can justify a discount, but only when the scope really becomes easier to maintain over time.
pages.metaTitle: Recurring cleaning pricing workflow that protects your margin | Cleaning Pricing Calculator
pages.metaDescription: Recurring work can justify a discount, but only when the scope really becomes easier to maintain over time.

sections.heading: Key takeaway
paragraphs: Discount the rhythm, not the labor value.

sections.heading: Practical detail
paragraphs: Weekly and biweekly service usually reduce reset time, while monthly work can still feel closer to a light deep clean.

sections.heading: Next action
paragraphs: Use the frequency selector to see how the working range changes before you offer the recurring rate.

sections.heading: Key takeaway
paragraphs: Clear extras are easier to sell and easier to protect.

sections.heading: Practical detail
paragraphs: Fridge interiors, oven degreasing, inside windows, and laundry extras all carry time that disappears when buried in the base rate.

sections.heading: Next action

pages.title: Move-out cleaning estimate workflow for higher-expectation jobs
pages.description: Empty homes often look simple, but they expose every missed detail and tend to carry tighter client expectations.

sections.heading: Key takeaway
paragraphs: Move-out pricing needs a bigger labor buffer than maintenance cleaning.

sections.heading: Practical detail
paragraphs: The lack of furniture does not automatically make the work easier when the condition is rough or the turnover deadline is tight.

sections.heading: Next action
paragraphs: Use the move-out option in the estimator and check the range against your condition notes.

sections.heading: When standard cleaning wins
paragraphs: Use a standard-cleaning rate when the home is already maintained and the visit mainly protects the current condition.

sections.heading: When deep cleaning wins
paragraphs: Use a higher deep-cleaning rate when the home needs reset work, heavier detail attention, or buildup removal.

sections.heading: Decision shortcut
paragraphs: If the home needs reset labor before maintenance can begin, price it as a deep clean.

pages.title: One-time vs recurring cleaning pricing and where the discount belongs
pages.description: One-time work and recurring work create different labor patterns, so the quote should reflect the rhythm instead of flattening everything.
pages.heroTitle: One-time vs recurring cleaning pricing and where the discount belongs
pages.heroIntro: One-time work and recurring work create different labor patterns, so the quote should reflect the rhythm instead of flattening everything.
pages.metaTitle: One-time vs recurring cleaning pricing and where the discount belongs | Cleaning Pricing Calculator
pages.metaDescription: One-time work and recurring work create different labor patterns, so the quote should reflect the rhythm instead of flattening everything.

sections.heading: When one-time cleaning wins
paragraphs: Use a higher one-time rate when each visit starts cold and the home may need more reset work.

sections.heading: When recurring cleaning wins
paragraphs: Use a recurring rate when consistent service lowers setup time and makes the home easier to maintain over time.

sections.heading: Decision shortcut
paragraphs: If repeated visits genuinely reduce effort, discount the recurring service slightly instead of discounting the one-time job.

pages.title: Flat-rate vs hourly cleaning pricing for small teams
pages.description: Both pricing models can work, but flat-rate quoting usually scales better once your scope math is consistent.
pages.heroTitle: Flat-rate vs hourly cleaning pricing for small teams
pages.heroIntro: Both pricing models can work, but flat-rate quoting usually scales better once your scope math is consistent.
pages.metaTitle: Flat-rate vs hourly cleaning pricing for small teams | Cleaning Pricing Calculator
pages.metaDescription: Both pricing models can work, but flat-rate quoting usually scales better once your scope math is consistent.

sections.heading: When flat-rate pricing wins
paragraphs: Use flat-rate pricing when you know the scope well enough to package the work cleanly and avoid constant time math during the quote.

sections.heading: When hourly pricing wins
paragraphs: Use hourly pricing when the scope is uncertain, access is messy, or the client keeps changing the work list.

sections.heading: Decision shortcut
paragraphs: Default to flat-rate when the scope is stable and switch to hourly when uncertainty is still too high.

sections.heading: What is inside
paragraphs: A plain-English checklist covering baseline math, service type, frequency, add-ons, minimum pricing, and quote follow-up.

sections.heading: How to use it
paragraphs: Start with the estimator, check the scope decisions, confirm add-ons, and copy the summary before sending the quote.

sections.heading: Best follow-up
paragraphs: Pair the checklist with the estimator so your quote rhythm stays consistent across inquiries.

sections.heading: Download it now
paragraphs: The resource is delivered as a plain-text checklist so it stays portable across notes apps, CRMs, project trackers, and print workflows.

paragraphs: This route stays static-first in the initial release, but it still defines the exact prompts worth collecting once the winning asset earns a lightweight form integration.

The real job of this page is operator clarity: if the site starts attracting repeat tool use or resource downloads, these prompts tell the next build exactly what friction is still slowing the user down.
